•Journal Article•[10.1001/JAMA.2010.1923](https://doi.org/10.1001/JAMA.2010.1923)

Gait speed and survival in older adults.
----------------------------------------

Stephanie A. Studenski,Subashan Perera,Kushang V. Patel,Caterina Rosano,Kimberly A. Faulkner,Marco Inzitari,Jennifer S. Brach,Julie Chandler,Peggy M. Cawthon,Elizabeth Connor,Michael C. Nevitt,Marjolein Visser,Stephen B. Kritchevsky,Stefania Badinelli,Tamara B. Harris,Anne B. Newman,Jane A. Cauley,Luigi Ferrucci,Jack M. Guralnik +18 moreUniversity of Pittsburgh,National Institutes of Health,Autonomous University of Barcelona,Merck & Co.,California Pacific Medical Center,University of California, San Diego,University of California, San Francisco,VU University Amsterdam,Wake Forest University

- 05 Jan 2011

- JAMA

Show Less

TL;DR: In this pooled analysis of individual data from 9 selected cohorts, gait speed was associated with survival in older adults and predicted survival was as accurate as predicted based on age, sex, use of mobility aids, and self-reported function.

TL;DR: Sarcopenia should be considered in all older patients who present with observed declines in physical function, strength, or overall health, and patients who meet these criteria should further undergo body composition assessment using dual energy x-ray absorptiometry with sarcopenia being defined using currently validated definitions.

TL;DR: Genetic loci associated with body mass index map near key hypothalamic regulators of energy balance, and one of these loci is near GIPR, an incretin receptor, which may provide new insights into human body weight regulation.
